So in baseball, when a pitcher throws four balls, that's a walk, so the batter goes to first base without the ball being hit. So the sentence "Juan Soto took ball four." would mean that during a plate appearance, the pitcher threw Juan Soto four balls, resulting in a walk. In baseball terminology, when a batter walks on four balls, it's common to say they "took a walk" or "walked on four balls." So saying "took ball four" is a bit of a shorthand or colloquial way of saying that the batter walked on four balls. Let me check if "took ball four" is an actual term used in baseball. In baseball, the count is often announced when a batter is close to a walk. For example, if a batter is on 3-0, the announcer might say "he's got three balls and no strikes." When a batter gets a ball that makes it 3-1, 3-2, and then a fourth ball, that's a walk. So when a batter is on 3-0, they might be said to "take a walk" if they don't swing at the next pitches and the pitcher throws balls to walk them. "Taking ball four" is a way of saying that a batter was walked on four balls, possibly without swinging at any pitches. It's a bit of a poetic or shorthand way of referring to a walk.
